data.title: Ulta Beauty: Strong Holiday Sales, And A Chance To Buy The Dip
data.description:
As we wrap up the Q4 earnings season, investors are seemingly looking for any and all reasons to sell off stocks. The ongoing war in the Middle East and the sharp disruption to oil prices, in particular, have driven intense selling pressure over the past few weeks, breaking the momentum of many stocks that had been rising over the past year.

Ulta Beauty (ULTA), the beauty products and cosmetics retailer, is one of the recent victims. The company just reported mixed Q4 results, leading to a ~10% drop post-earnings. Still, Ulta remains up more than 80% over the past year. The question for investors now is: is Ulta's rally finally breaking, or is now a good time to buy the dip in this momentum stock?

I last wrote a buy rating on Ulta in January, when the stock was trading at $620 per share. I'll cut to the chase here: I certainly wasn't 100% pleased by Ulta's most recent earnings print. While I'll give the company a pass on its perceived weak guidance (the company does have a tendency to start off conservatively), I am eyeing the company's recent margin slippage in Q4 with some apprehension. At the same time, however, I do like the fact that the company continues to gain market share and post strong top-line results, and amid the post-earnings fall to the ~$570s, I think Ulta's valuation remains appropriate relative to its earnings growth potential. I remain cautiously optimistic on this stock and am reiterating my buy rating on this name.

Let's address the elephant in the room first: Ulta's guidance for FY26, which is the primary reason the stock fell post-Q4. For FY26, Ulta has guided to 6-7% y/y revenue growth, which contains 2.5-3.5% y/y comp sales growth. As a reminder, comp sales growth measures the revenue growth from existing store locations and is the core growth metric that investors use to look at retail and restaurant companies' health, especially relative to their peers.

The company is also expecting 6-9% operating income growth, implying slight y/y margin expansion, and $28.05-$28.55 in pro forma EPS (+10% y/y). The top and bottom line outlooks were both a slight miss to Street expectations, which had hoped for 3.5% in comp sales growth and $28.58 in EPS.

That said, I hardly think the initial outlook is a reason to dump Ulta stock. We should trace back to Ulta's own guidance history: the company's original outlook for FY25 called for just 0-1% in comp sales growth and $22.50-$22.90 in pro forma EPS.

In reality, Ulta just closed out FY25 with 5.4% comp sales growth (3.3% pricing growth plus 2.0% transaction growth) and $25.64 in EPS, 13% above the company's original midpoint. Arguably, FY25 was also quite a volatile and unpredictable year, especially considering the macro fallout of tariffs. And yet Ulta still managed to deliver big beats to its original guidance. We expect a similar trend to play out for FY26.

We should also take a step back and refresh ourselves on the core growth drivers that Ulta unlocked in FY25 that set up the company for success in FY26. The company's launch of Ulta Beauty Marketplace, which is a third-party marketplace for brands to list their products directly to consumers, is a major upside driver in my eyes. The company has launched 200 new brands on the UB marketplace and listed over 5,000 unique products, while the company's UB Media Ads product will also bring in direct advertising revenue from brands.

These core initiatives will not only help to diversify and grow Ulta's revenue stream but also give it "free" high-margin sources of revenue that can combat recent declines.

All in all, while Q4 was a mixed quarter, I'm happy to hold my Ulta shares through the volatility and bank on a rebound.

Q4 recap

Let's now go through Ulta's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Ulta's revenue grew 12% y/y to $3.90 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $3.83 (+10% y/y) by a two-point margin. Revenue growth kept pace with 13% y/y growth in Q3. Meanwhile, as shown in the chart below, Ulta's comparable sales clocked in at 5.8% y/y growth. This was slightly softer than 6.4% y/y growth in Q3 and 6.7% y/y growth in Q2. That said, we do note that Ulta's ability to exit FY25 at a ~6% comps growth pace makes the company's guidance calling for just ~3% comps growth in FY26 feel like an attainable target.

Ulta's recent sales strength has been driven by a number of factors. Product newness and strong merchandising have been core to the recent sales lift, with the company noting that it onboarded 100+ new brands in 2025. Also, the company has continued to prioritize in-store pop-up events, which is how many consumers discover new brands for the first time. In 2025, the company hosted over 100k in-store events, which also strengthened the company's omnichannel appeal and differentiated it from purely e-commerce beauty companies. Ulta notes that it gained market share in the mass and prestige beauty segments.

Specifically, the company also notes that fragrance was the company's best-selling product category in the quarter, driving double-digit comps growth, with makeup following on its heels. Per CFO Christopher DelOrifice's remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

Fragrance was the strongest performing category again this quarter, delivering double-digit comp growth fueled by newness from established brands, including YSL and Prada as well as exclusive brands such as NOYZ, Snif, and Summer Mink by Drake, coupled with strong performance of holiday gift sets. New co-branded TV campaigns, expanded space in stores and better in-stocks successfully positioned Ulta Beauty as the fragrance destination this holiday season. The hair care category delivered its best comp performance this year with comp growth for the quarter in the high single-digit range, primarily driven by strong performance from new brands, including amika and Moroccanoil and exclusive Cecred, which continued to build sales momentum after a fantastic launch in April.

The skin care and Wellness category delivered mid-single-digit comp growth driven by prestige, skin care and wellness. K-Beauty brands, medicube, ANUA, and Peach & Lily, and new brands, DRMTLGY and Personal Day drove strong guest engagement, while highly giftable launches from Therabody, Nodpod and Saje, which is exclusive to Ulta Beauty, delivered strong growth in wellness. We took market share in the makeup category with low single-digit growth in total, supported by positive comps in both mass and prestige makeup."

It's on the margin front where Ulta delivered some concerning news. Gross margins slipped -10bps yesterday/y to 38.1%. The company attributed the decline to an unfavorable channel mix and dis-economies of scale in fixed store costs. Meanwhile, corporate overhead (SG&A) costs rose 23% y/y, far outpacing revenue growth on the heels of higher performance-based incentive compensation and higher sales and marketing spend to support in-store events. As a percentage of revenue, SG&A rose 230bps year/y to 25.7% of revenue.

As a result, operating income actually fell -8% y/y despite double-digit top-line growth, as operating margins shrunk -260bps y/y. Pro forma EPS of $8.01 in the quarter also fell -5% y/y and slightly missed Street expectations of $8.03.

Risks, valuation, and key takeaways

Of course, we can't ignore the risk of Ulta continuing to shed its margin profile. Its gross margins in the high 30s are already quite slim and don't leave much room for further compression. We should also watch out for intensifying macro pressures in 2026 and their potential impact on Ulta's comp sales trends.

That said, I think there are mitigating factors as well. We continue to look to UB Marketplace not only as a way for Ulta to drive incremental revenue from brands that don't exist in the company's stores but also as a substantial catalyst for margin accretion.

At post-earnings share prices near $570, Ulta trades at a 20x FY26 P/E, which is neither cheap nor expensive. Again, however, I do think the company has a strong chance to outperform its conservative initial guidance (recall FY25 EPS outperformed by 13%), so I think this is an attractive dip to buy.

data.title: Oil Above $100: Why Game Theory Suggests This Spike Won't Last
data.description:
Introduction

Since the US and Israel started striking Iran on February 28th, markets have reacted poorly. The S&P 500 is down 3%, the Dow Jones index is down 5.6%, and crude oil prices are up over 50%. In this article, I examine the actions of the various belligerents from a Game Theory perspective to attempt to forecast a timeline of the likely resolution of this conflict and what it means for investments. I conclude that both sides have a strong incentive to return to cooperation before long, which would likely entail a mean reversion in both oil prices and some of the stocks hardest hit at the start of this conflict.

Why Attack Makes Strategic Sense for the US and Israel

Before I start this analysis, I ask readers to put their political and geopolitical views to the side, if possible. These days everything gets politicized, but I think we can make greater sense of the actions of each side here purely by examining them through a strategic lens.

Before the start of this war, Iran’s strategy was very similar to the strategy used by North Korea in the past. North Korea has over 8500 field artillery and thousands of missile launchers pointed at our ally, South Korea, plus nuclear weapons. The North Korean regime has successfully made it too costly for the United States or its allies to consider an attack as a strategy. 73 years after the armistice that ended the fighting on the Korean peninsula, North and South Korea are technically still in a state of war. This state of play is called a “frozen conflict” - a “limbo” equilibrium in which neither side pursues cooperation or conflict.

For the regime in Iran, the North Korean playbook is an instruction manual for immunizing itself from Western threats. The first step would be to build up a devastating stockpile of conventional weapons and then use that stockpile as a deterrence shield while developing nuclear weapons.

The difference, though, is that North Korea doesn’t also fund terrorist proxies in South Korea, Japan, Taiwan, and the rest of the region. With a massive conventional stockpile and nuclear weapons, Iran would be capable of funding its proxies around the region while protected from Israeli or American retaliation.

So for the US and Israel, the clock was ticking. Strategically, attacking now made complete sense. The longer they waited, the more costly the attack would become. This strategy is known as “preventive” warfare (as opposed to “preemptive” warfare). The dynamic is often at play between a rising power and a dominant hegemon. The hegemon will frequently decide to pursue an advantageous war to cripple the rise of its rival rather than accept a disadvantageous peace later on. Although Iran would not be a global rival of the United States any time soon, the same logic applies to this dynamic. If we do nothing and wait for Iran’s strategy to bear fruit, we get to a state approximating mutually assured destruction (akin to the situation between North and South Korea). Therefore, it is better to attack now. For the US and Israel, war today is worth it to avoid a MAD-style stalemate tomorrow.

Iran’s Strategy So Far

I would argue that Iran’s strategy, both after the attacks last year and during this year’s conflict, has closely resembled a “Tit for Tat” strategy at the state level. In Game Theory, a “Tit for Tat” strategy is characterized by quick retaliation but also quick forgiveness. If the opponent attacks in the prior round, you attack in this round. But if the opponent returns to cooperation, you quickly reciprocate by cooperating as well. As an example, when the US killed Qasem Soleimani in January 2020, Iran vowed to retaliate and did so by targeting US bases in the Middle East with missile strikes. At the time, markets briefly panicked - equities fell and oil rose. The US responded with additional sanctions but didn’t escalate militarily. The same pattern played out again during the brief confrontation in June 2025.

In 1980, Professor Robert Axelrod sponsored a tournament to test various strategies in a game of repeated “prisoners’ dilemmas” (each game was 200 rounds of the prisoners’ dilemma). Whichever strategy was able to rack up the most points across many games versus opponents utilizing all kinds of strategies was the “winner.” It turned out that the “Tit for Tat” strategy performed the best out of all strategies tested. Although the “Tit for Tat” strategy didn’t win against every individual opponent strategy, it performed the best across many games because it minimized damage to itself. It is essentially a “be nice, but don’t be a pushover” strategy. The optimal strategy has four properties: it is nice, retaliatory, forgiving, and clear. This strategy works well because it both deters exploitation and quickly returns to cooperation after an exchange of conflict.

If I’m right and Iran continues employing the “Tit for Tat” strategy, there is plenty of reason to believe that the conflict will be relatively short-lived. The one uncertainty, in my view, is the new Supreme Leader. Whether Mojtaba Khomeini behaves as a “rational actor” after the death of his father, wife, and son at the hands of the US and Israel is, of course, somewhat unknown. Admittedly, personal vendettas could prevent rational behavior in this conflict. But the Revolutionary Guard and the institutions surrounding the Supreme Leader make the country’s behavior more predictably rational, in my view.

One thing worth noting is that while Iran itself plays a “Tit for Tat” strategy, it funds groups that play less cooperative strategies. So overall, Iran attempts to prevent harm to itself by playing a “Tit for Tat” strategy while funding players who are using non-cooperative strategies against Iran’s main opponents.

Assuming Iran’s strategy remains the same, it is likely that the regime will return to cooperation and negotiation after the current round of aggression is concluded. The major reason to expect this outcome is the existence of substantial common interests between Iran, Israel, the US, and the entire world.

Common Interest - the Strait of Hormuz

Thomas Schelling’s pivotal work “The Strategy of Conflict” showed that antagonists can arrive at a peaceful equilibrium if there is a “common interest.” Schelling said that in the landscape of potential outcomes there is often a “focal point” that both sides understand even absent communication. In a famous example, he asked people at what time and place they would meet a stranger in New York City if both individuals knew they needed to meet the other, but neither person was able to communicate with the other beforehand. If you said noon at Grand Central Terminal, you would likely have guessed the meeting point correctly.

If there is a focal point in all this, it’s undoubtedly the Strait of Hormuz. This waterway is like the carotid artery of the global economy. As of now, Iran has “closed” the strait to vessels other than its own, while Iranian oil exports through the strait have barely been halted at all. The US is wary about stopping or seizing Iranian oil exports for a few reasons. First, doing so would mean Iran no longer has anything left to lose. It would devastate what is left of Iran’s economy. Iran could respond by laying mines all throughout the strait, rendering it unnavigable for months or years. Second, doing so would provoke China. Right now, China has every incentive to pressure Iran to open the strait to other countries’ oil exports. Over half of China’s oil imports flow through the Strait of Hormuz, with about 14% of total oil imports coming from Iran. If the US blocked Iran’s oil exports, China would likely replace its lost supply through greater purchases from Russia, which would improve Russia’s hand in the Ukraine conflict. So, in my view, the US is confident that international pressure will force Iran’s hand off of oil tankers moving through the strait.

Everyone has a powerful incentive to make sure the waterway remains open. The diplomatic solution is obvious: the US and Israel stop attacking, Iran allows oil exports through the strait to resume, and everyone gets back to the negotiating table. To optimize its strategy, Iran has to inflict some pain on the US and Israel (to make the “tit” in its strategy worth actually worrying about) but quickly resume cooperation afterward.

The Game of Chicken

Thomas Schelling also analyzed geopolitical conflicts as complicated games of “chicken” in which two cars are racing toward each other and each one has to decide whether or not to swerve to avoid the other. The person to swerve first is the “chicken”. International conflicts usually don’t end with a strict “winner” and a “chicken”. What happens more often is escalation, followed by loud enunciations of resolve and then quiet, implicit coordination toward a resolution (toward the “focal point” mentioned earlier). Schelling emphasized the importance of allowing the opponent a face-saving off-ramp.

To move toward a resolution, Iran and the US/Israel both need to be able to claim some kind of a victory. The US and Israel have to be careful to avoid a situation in which Iran feels like it cannot credibly claim victory or walk away with its head held high. Although it can be tempting to humiliate and destroy an opponent, it is a poor strategy to pursue if the goal is to return to diplomacy and negotiation later on. What I believe this looks like as an outcome is that Iran continues its attacks for a short period after Israeli and US attacks cease, and then the Iranian regime can claim that the US and Israel “gave up,” while the US and Israel can claim that they accomplished their objectives.

Investment Implications

So what does all this mean from an investment perspective? Right now, I believe the markets are pricing in an extended conflict, when in fact theory and history suggest that the conflict will likely be transient. If the conflict resolves and the Strait of Hormuz reopens sooner than expected, I believe it will result in a quick reversion toward the mean for several asset classes:

XLE and USO

The Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLE) is up 26% year-to-date, while the United States Oil Fund (USO) is up nearly 72% year-to-date. The move is comparable to what happened after Russia invaded Ukraine (incidentally exactly four years ago). It took years for oil prices to return to pre-Ukraine war levels, but I think the geopolitics in Ukraine vs. Iran are fundamentally different. The US and Israel are extremely unlikely to pursue a ground invasion of Iran the way Russia has in Ukraine. Absent a ground invasion, there are only so many sites to be bombed in Iran. Similarly, there are only so many targets Iran can attack with its missiles and drones in the region. That naturally puts a limit on the timeline of the conflict in my view. So I think that once the conflict ends, we’ll see a quick return to normalcy in oil prices and in energy/oil ETFs.

JETS

Since the start of the conflict, the US Global Jets ETF (JETS) is down over 18%. Airline stocks tend to be extremely hard-hit by oil price rises, since rising oil prices significantly hit their already low margins. If a resolution leads to a mean reversion in oil prices, then airline stocks could be poised to revert to their pre-war levels.

S&P 500 and SPY/IVV/VOO

One of my prior articles using simulation analysis predicted a high probability of a 10% correction in the S&P 500 before the midterm election this year, but a low probability of a bear market resulting from it. In my view, this crisis could be a catalyst for that kind of correction in the short term, particularly if the conflict remains unresolved for several weeks. A resolution could then be a catalyst for a market bottom, in my view. For now I continue to rate the index and the ETFs tracking it a "Hold".

A Few More Weeks of Pain?

President Trump claimed that airstrikes in Iran would take “four weeks or less” to fully accomplish our objectives. We’re about two weeks in so far, which (if you believe Trump’s timelines) leaves another two weeks of strikes still to go. Press Secretary Karoline Levitt suggested a timeline of six weeks. Although the President has a penchant for overly-optimistic forecasts, I think four to six weeks is a believable timeframe in this conflict. That timeline could leave another two to four weeks of pain for equity markets.

I think it is quite likely that the conflict continues to damage global equities until there is a clear light at the end of the tunnel. Therefore, I forecast a few more weeks of decline in equity markets and rising oil prices before resolution leads to a relatively quick reversal.

Final Thoughts

Keep in mind that headlines like “World War 3 is Upon Us” sell papers and get clicks, but predictions of the sky falling rarely turn out to be correct. In this case, the path of least resistance for all parties involved seems to be toward a quick resolution with the Strait of Hormuz open. A protracted war suits no one’s interests. Therefore, I believe it is important for investors not to overreact to dramatic headlines but to be ready to act at the right moment. A few more weeks of conflict could offer a good “buy the dip” opportunity for the broad indexes and particularly hard-hit stocks and a selling opportunity for XLE and USO, in my view.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: The Start Of The 'War'
data.description:
The United States attacked Iran on Saturday, February 28, 2026.

The first week of Federal Reserve statistics that covered any of the time period that includes the beginning of the bombing is the banking week ending March 4, 2026.

That just covers the past 11 days in the last two 'banking weeks.'

The portfolio of securities held outright rose by just under $30.0 billion.

The Total Factor Supply of reserves within the commercial banking system rose by $33.0 billion. So, the rise in the securities portfolio represented most of this rise in the factor supply of commercial banks.

Overall, the Reserve Balances held by the commercial banking system rose by about $63.0 billion.

The biggest contributor to this last item was a decline in bank deposits that were held at the Fed. These balances declined by just under $43.0 billion. This number basically records the change in the amount of commercial bank money that was held in deposits within the Federal Reserve System.

As commercial banks move money from the Federal Reserve back into the commercial banking system, cash balances increase within the commercial banks.

So, in the latest banking week, these items accounted for a $66.4 billion cash flow from the Federal Reserve to the commercial banks.

In other words, the Fed's balance sheet shows that a substantial amount of cash was transferred into the commercial banking system in the first week and one-half of the "war" taking place in the Middle East.

At the start of December 2025, the Federal Reserve had been engaged in a policy path of quantitative easing.

After three years and three-quarters of a year in which the Federal Reserve had engaged in a policy of quantitative easing, the central bank had finally moved away from its policy of quantitative tightening.

From November 26, 2025, through March 4, 2026, the Federal Reserve had increased its securities portfolio by $102.1 billion.

During this time period, the Fed also oversaw a movement of $35.9 billion from deposits at Federal Reserve banks back to the vaults of member commercial banks.

Overall, Reserve Balances with Federal Reserve banks had increased by $115.7 billion during this time period, from November 26, 2025, to March 4, 2026.

Reserve Balances with Federal Reserve banks have now increased by $175.2 billion from November 26, 2025, to March 11, 2026.

Here is the chart.

The behavior of the effective Federal Funds rate over this specific time period. Remember, Federal Reserve policy is now moving the effective Federal Funds rate to follow its lead in terms of how it is managing its securities portfolio.

Currently, the Federal Open Market Committee (the FOMC) appears to be reluctant to lower the Federal Funds rate any further at this time.

This is particularly true after March 6, when concerns over future inflation have further clouded the picture of the future.

So, the Federal Reserve is now caught between two pictures of the future.

The first picture is the one that the Fed had been working with since the start of December 2025.

The Fed has just begun its new policy stance of quantitative easing.

The general picture the Fed presented was of a monetary policy that was adding securities to its securities portfolio and looking forward to letting the commercial banking system go and modestly supporting a rising level of economic activity.

The real economy was growing in the 2.0% to 2.5% range, year-over-year.

Unemployment was in the 4.0% to 4.5% range, not too far above what is labeled "full" employment.

And the inflation rate was around 2.5%, higher than the Federal Reserve wants, but not too far above a 2.0% target.

So, the economy has been in reasonable condition, could be better, but it is in a state that could be acceptable to many policymakers and to many politicians.

The second picture is one where the federal deficits are rising to support the spending on the "war." This picture includes some other possible conflicts and some other support of military spending.

The added spending would represent a major commitment to further spending and to future deficits, policy impacts that would require a greater coordination of the monetary and fiscal policy aspects of the government's responsibilities.

The underlying picture coming from this scenario is one of Federal Reserve support of the government spending programs and a Federal Reserve stance of helping to finance and underwrite the government deficits that would be relatively large and relatively steady.

The underlying picture here is one of rising inflation that accompanies large budget deficits that are just a part of the daily activity of the Federal government.

The environment created by the current approach to monetary policy would end, and the Federal Reserve would act in a more supportive way to assure the funding of the larger, more regular deficits.

The Federal Reserve would probably need to revert to a policy focus dominated by the management of the Fed's policy rate of interest. Times would be more like the end of the 2000s rather than those of this century.

It would be a return, however partial, to the environment of the 19th century.

Interest rates would be the signal…not the response.

One other part of the monetary policy picture. This is the part connected with the management of the Money Stock.

Recently, the behavior of the M2 money stock has been much more controlled. Here, take a look at going back to January 2025.

Note that the expansion of the M2 money stock is pretty steady. This is a nonseasonally adjusted series and so would tend to be bumpy.

But the rate of expansion appears to be pretty steady.

Economists believe that this is the result of the steady behavior of the Federal Reserve and the "trust" that the Federal Reserve has built up within the banking and financial communities during the last 15 years or so. As market participants build greater trust with policymakers, their behavior becomes more predictable. Trust is a good thing to share in financial and banking markets.

I think the performance of U.S. markets over the past 15 years or so speaks to this.

I believe that if the relationship changes here in the money and financial markets, the trust that now exists between the Federal Reserve and market participants will decline, and the statistics we see, like the one above, charting the behavior of the M2 money stock, will show less and less stability.

These more variable statistics will just reflect how much more difficult it will be to work in markets that are more predictable and more trustworthy.

We will, I believe, miss this if we move to a more volatile environment.

Our first job now is to watch the monetary statistics closely and see what is happening…what changes are being made.

The Federal Reserve seems to be supporting the "war effort" by putting a lot of reserves into the banking system.

We must, in essence, begin to learn all over again.

data.title: UPRO: Use Leverage To Reduce Risk, Here's How
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SPY) has detached from its all-time highs reached only a few days ago and is now trading 4% below peak levels (see chart below). The catalyst for the quick pullback may have been the Middle East crisis. But underneath the surface, other factors probably contributed to risk asset investors (even long-term treasuries (TLT) (VGLT) and gold (GLD) have also sunk 4% or 5% since the start of March) rushing to the exits:

  • Stubborn inflation.
  • Weakness in key job metrics.
  • Pessimism about the direction of interest rates.
  • Crude oil and the US dollar dominating the safe-haven trade.
  • Richly valued stocks.

The war in Iran and the surrounding area may have been merely the straw that is starting to bend the camel's back.

What if I told you that one proposed solution to play defense now, without giving up much exposure to the upside, should the markets rebound, is to own the ProShares UltraPro S&P500 ETF (UPRO), a 3x leveraged S&P 500 fund? I'll explore below how this can be done.

About UPRO

UPRO is a fairly simple ETF to understand: it aims at producing three times the daily returns of SPY, both to the upside and downside. Aside from a bit of tracking error, the fund has done a great job meeting its stated goal since its mid-2009 inception.

The gains or losses compound daily since the ETF resets at the conclusion of each trading session. This process adds fuel to the fire: if the S&P 500 is ripping higher, UPRO may deliver even more than three times the performance of the index over a multiple-day period (see one cherry-picked example below of the March 2020 to June 2021 period, when UPRO more than quintupled the cumulative gains of the S&P 500). More often than not, however, the benchmark will ebb and flow without climbing sharply, which tends to hurt the longer-term performance of UPRO due to volatility drag (read this post from the SEC for more information).

There is no question that the main purpose of UPRO is for very short-term traders to capture the eventual daily gains produced by the US stock index. ProShares itself states, on the ETF's page, that "for any holding period other than a day, your return may be higher or lower than the Daily Target. These differences may be significant."

That said, I believe that leveraged ETFs are severely misunderstood. While volatility drag is a real headwind to portfolio performance over time, it does not mean that leveraged ETFs like UPRO are "broken" in any way and that some market maker on Wall Street is ripping off the fund's shareholders. Instead, it is just a function of how high volatility works when a fund resets its positions every day.

For instance, notice below how I can very closely replicate the performance of the S&P 500 over many years by simply deleveraging UPRO and mixing it at a ratio of 33/67 with cash (rebalanced quarterly, in this case)—some drag to performance can be attributed to UPRO's richer annual expense ratio of 89 bps. This is a vital concept for the lower-risk, potentially higher-return strategy that I am about to propose.

For clarity and to re-emphasize the point above before we move forward, a 3x leveraged fund like UPRO is substantially riskier than a non-leveraged fund like SPY when held as a stand-alone security. When de-risked by two-thirds to mimic SPY, it effectively behaves like SPY (chart above), including in what pertains to it being held over longer than a single day.

Therefore, from a risk-mitigation standpoint, one-third UPRO plus two-thirds cash should be treated, by and large, like an investment in SPY. Any stop-loss strategy or trading decision based on technical analysis that an investor might choose to use with SPY would be equally valid for a one-third UPRO, two-thirds cash position.

(Those interested can visit my profile to check my past coverage of UPRO for more information about the ETF).

Using UPRO Wisely

So, back to the first few paragraphs, how can one use leverage to reduce risk (but not eliminate it altogether) amid the current market turmoil, which could mean further weakness in US stock prices? The key here is to de-lever UPRO to mimic the performance of the S&P 500 if held at 100% allocation, more specifically by allocating 33% to the 3x fund, as done in the previous section above, and then using the freed-up capital of 67% to invest in hedging instruments.

By hedging instruments, I mean funds or securities in general that tend to (1) have very low or negative correlation with the S&P 500 while (2) producing non-negative returns over time. The best candidates for desirable hedging instruments to own, in my view, are the following (I will include an honorable mention at the end):

Managed Future ETFs

I have just written about the KraneShares Mount Lucas Managed Futures Index Strategy ETF (KMLM) and its peers, the Simplified Managed Futures Strategy ETF (CTA) and the iMGP DBi Managed Futures Strategy ETF (DBMF). Trend following has proven, over a multi-decade period, to be an outstanding diversifier to stocks and bonds while, when done right, generally producing positive returns.

Long-Short Low Beta

Another of my favorite hedges is the AGF U.S. Market Neutral Anti-Beta Fund ETF (BTAL), which is a strategy that goes short on high-beta stocks and long on low-beta ones. The ETF has historically broken even, give or take one or two percentage points per year. Many investors might not like this ETF because it is not designed to add much or at all to a portfolio's gains over time. However, it does wonders at diversifying risk away due to the negative correlations (see table below).

Treasuries

The honorable mention goes to treasuries. Traditionally, investment-grade corporate and government bonds have served as a popular complement to stocks. However, due to the spike in inflation and interest rates in 2022, and considering today's stagflation worries, more investors have grown skeptical of this asset class as a powerful diversifier—notice below the uncomfortably high correlation coefficient of +0.62 between TLT and SPY since 2022.

So, here is a proposed quarterly-rebalanced portfolio to possibly weather 2026 more smoothly without giving up much upside opportunity:

  • 33% UPRO (to emulate 100% SPY).
  • 17% KMLM.
  • 17% CTA.
  • 17% DBMF.
  • 16% BTAL.

Of course, I don't know exactly how this portfolio will fare going forward. But at least historically, this is how the strategy would have performed over the past few years of generally climbing stock prices: (1) about the same annual return as the S&P 500, give or take 20 bps; (2) lower volatility by three percentage points; (3) noticeably higher Sortino ratio (risk-adjusted performance); and (4) about half the maximum drawdown.

What Could Happen Next

Should the S&P 500 rip higher from here, recovering sharply from the recent war-driven pullback, it is likely that the strategy above will not quite outperform the stock index—although I bet it would still produce strong returns, driven in part by that beneficial "melt up" effect on leveraged funds that I discussed above, but also depending on how the trend-following strategy might perform.

If, instead, the S&P 500 continues to crater, there is a higher likelihood that the blended portfolio above would outperform due to its more defensive nature, even if it does not necessarily produce strong, positive returns.

To be sure I am properly covering the risks, as a third scenario, there is always the possibility that the losses of the portfolio would be larger than those of SPY alone. For it to be true, the S&P 500 and the trend-following ETFs collectively would have to accumulate losses together (BTAL would almost certainly not, since short high-beta is more of a pure, natural hedge against stock market declines).

Historically, trend following does best when stocks are in extended decline (see 2022, early 2024, and early 2025 below). In the very improbable scenario of all ETFs heading lower, the fact that the portfolio is effectively leveraged by a factor of 2x would likely lead to steeper losses than those produced by SPY alone.

data.title: Beta Bionics: Speculative Appeal After An Eventful Start To The Year
data.description:
In December, I believed that Beta Bionics, Inc. (BBNX) had seen an interesting year. Real momentum was seen in the business and the share price. Few would have expected a pullback to the tune of two-thirds in just about a quarter's time, with shares having plunged from $30 in December to levels around the $10 mark today.

The maker of the iLet product line provides algorithm-based insulin administration for patients diagnosed with Type 1 diabetes, and more recently the solution is used by Type 2 diabetes patients as well. Concerns about slower patient intake, revenues already growing because of a larger Type 2 patient population, a softer outlook for 2026, and an FDA warning letter spooked investors.

This makes it so that shares have seen a massive setback, and while the fundamentals have been impacted, the risk-reward has improved, with the focus on the word speculative here.

I'm usually attracted to large pullbacks, some of which are covered in greater detail at Value In Corporate Events, but I'm holding back on this one.

Rattling Investors

Shares of Beta Bionics dropped from $30 to $20 overnight in early January when the company posted preliminary fourth-quarter results. This came even as preliminary revenues were seen up 56% to $32.0 million. Growth was supported by a 23% increase in Durable Medical Equipment sales to $22.3 million, while Pharmacy Benefit Plan revenues more than tripled to $9.7 million.

Some 35,000 patients are included in the installed customer care, after just over 5,500 patient starts were reported in the quarter, including an estimated 25-30% share of type 2 diabetes patients.

Shares fell to the mid-teens in the middle of February when the definitive 2025 results were announced. Reported numbers were in line with the preliminary numbers, as the company ended up posting fourth-quarter operating losses of $16.1 million on $32.1 million in sales. All this looks favorable in comparison to just over a $100 million revenue number for the year, with operating losses reported at $71 million.

Investors, however, were unimpressed by the 2026 guidance, calling for sales between $130 and $135 million, after annualized revenues trended at $128 million in the fourth quarter. Gross margins are seen at 56.5% of sales, plus or minus a point; this is up a point from the full year 2025 performance.

This suggests continued operational losses, these being substantial in the grand scheme of things, as momentum is clearly coming down. This is a concern, certainly as investors shopped for higher growth given the introduction to the Type 2 diabetes market.

Valuations Implode

Down to just $10 per share, a share count of 44 million values equity at $440 million. This actually includes about $265 million in cash, equivalents, and short-term investments, implying that operating asset valuations have dropped well below $200 million here, at just over 1 times sales. With operating losses trending at around $64 million each year, net cash holdings are equal to about 4 times the current operating loss rate, with the company benefiting from net interest income reducing net losses meanwhile.

These valuations are very depressed, as the business went public at $17 in the spring of last year, and after initially trading around the $10 mark, shares moved up to the $30 mark towards year-end. Those peak levels represented an operating asset valuation of around a billion, or about 9 times annualized sales.

All of this was driven by strong growth of the iLet product line-up, hopes for the Type 2 diabetes market being catered to, and stronger growth reported as the year progressed.

Expectations Come In

Between December and today, shares are down two-thirds, which, given a substantial net cash position, means that the roughly 10 times sales multiple has dropped to just over 1 times, an indication of the valuation implosion taking place here. This started following the release of the preliminary fourth-quarter results, as the 2026 guidance feels very soft, or at best is conservative, with hardly any growth seen from the current run rate in terms of sales.

This furthermore does not bode well for operating leverage, as operating losses are substantial, with much more work to be done on that front, although the company has solid net cash holdings.

Investors are spooked about slightly slower new patient numbers in the fourth quarter, but mostly because of the guidance for 2026, which was not called conservative on the conference call. This comes as a great portion of sales are seen moving through the pharmacy channel, resulting in relatively lower revenues, but still, all this feels rather soft, or hopefully conservative.

Investors were furthermore spooked to learn that the company obtained a warning letter from the FDA in January, although the executives sounded confident that this would be handled. Moreover, no competitive dynamics are seen changing in 2026, that of course being the prime concern with competitive offerings potentially being superior over time.

Shares have been pressured, driven by this cocktail of bad news, as the recent IPO of formerly Medtronic's MiniMed Group (MMED) was far from a winner as well. This much larger $2.7 billion peer trades at less than 2 times sales, and while sales grew in the low double digits, it reports some losses as well, although much more manageable losses (relatively) compared to Beta Bionics.

Speculative

Frankly, the situation now is that of speculative appeal. Following the momentum building up last year, the pullback has been severe, certainly factoring in the net cash holdings.

Right now the valuations look de-risked from that perspective, and while 2026 might become a tougher year, it might very well turn out to be conservative as well. Right now the risk-reward has improved, if only for the fact that shares sold off so much, although speculative remains the right word in the case of appeal here.

data.title: Nebius: Why Nvidia's Investment Isn't A Guarantee Of Future Returns
data.description:
Introduction

Since my last article, in which I rated Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) as a Sell, the stock price is up significantly. This was largely due to a partnership with Nvidia (NVDA), which helped the stock to recover after missing all of the expectations in the latest earnings.

I’ve been following this stock since my latest coverage in December, including the earnings, their recent presentation at the Morgan Stanley conference, and all of the other news, including the new NVDA partnership. After reviewing everything, which I’ll share with you today, I still think the risk/reward ratio isn’t compelling. The good news is largely priced in, although the risks I was largely concerned about in the previous piece are getting addressed, and for this, I am upgrading my rating from Sell to a Hold. Partnership with Nvidia, while definitely a large win for Nebius, doesn’t tell us much about where the stock of this company will be in the future, even though I know many investors see this as a confirmation of the resilience of Nebius.

The Nvidia Partnership

Let’s start directly with the biggest news. Nvidia is planning a $2 billion investment into Nebius as part of a strategic collaboration, and I know that this looks like a massive validation for the market, but as a long-time Nvidia shareholder, I look at this partnership slightly more realistically. Shares of Nebius went up 10% on the news, but Nvidia stock stayed flat because this is not a big deal for this company.

To keep things in perspective, let’s look at Nvidia. It currently has over $62 billion in cash. For them, a $2 billion investment is just another bet, which would be nice if it works out, but if not, it’s not a big deal. They have so much capital that they are looking for places to invest it to make sure their ecosystem grows, even if, along the way, some of the investments like this one will prove to be useless.

While I am not implying this specific bet will not be a winning one, I have an example of a stock that wasn’t saved from plummeting by collaboration with Nvidia. Many investors argued that because Nvidia partnered with Super Micro Computer (SMCI) and gave them early access to chips, the stock was "safe,” and it had only one way to go. In fact, I was heavily criticized on this platform for my Sell ratings of SMCI, and the reason given was often Nvidia. But a partnership doesn't protect you from internal execution failures or other issues. SMCI is down to $30 per share, and all of my Sell ratings proved to be correct over time.

Now that we’ve settled that, let’s look at the partnership and how, with this, the relationship between these two companies has changed. In the past, a company like Nebius was basically a customer of Nvidia. They ordered chips, waited for them to arrive, and then put them to use in various ways, each company making its own system. But with this deal, instead of being a customer who is getting an end product, Nebius becomes a co-designer of Nvidia in a way, and especially now, it creates a unique moat for NBIS with Vera Rubin chips coming out soon. Vera Rubin is supposed to become the crown jewel of the next AI cycle, and Nebius now has direct exposure to this new technology.

I know the market is worried that the adoption of Vera Rubin will not be as fast as that of Blackwell because of how much money was already spent by companies on those chips. At the same time, it looks like Vera Rubin is not just a more powerful version of the old tech. The Rubin platform integrates a new "Vera" processor, which will be processing the "thinking" logic of AI, and pairs it with the actual graphics processing power we're used to. Going into details, to explain it better, Nvidia here shifts from selling just very strong GPUs that can calculate enormous data loads to making something similar to an AI, which can “think” about the result and make some kind of a decision. I am saying “thinks” and “some kind,” because this is not what AI does today. It is essentially a powerful pattern recognizer, but that’s a story for another article.

Returning to Vera Rubin, in the past, a GPU was a huge math machine that could do millions of repetitive calculations (matrix multiplications) simultaneously, and that power was used to generate images or text. However, now as we move into the era of "Agentic AI," where we want software to understand and decide things (again, not really, but that’s how we call it) and make other tasks that would help or even substitute humans, being this huge math machine is not enough. So Nvidia introduced the "Vera" processor (a CPU), which will be the brain of this math machine, which is a GPU. This way, we not only get a powerful chip on which you can train models by extensive calculations and pattern recognition, but you also get a synchronized system in which Nvidia has removed the communication bottlenecks that slowed down previous generations.

For an investor, this means Nebius will have access to the coordinated system specifically built to handle the sophisticated reasoning required for the next generation of AI agents, and it allows them to build their data centers from the beginning with the unique cooling and power needs of these chips before the broader market can get them.

Besides, Nvidia and Nebius go full into "Agentic AI." They will be building a specific software stack for these agents, and it supposedly should help them be faster and much smoother. This means a deeper integration than we’ve seen before because Nvidia’s software libraries and models will be optimized specifically for the Nebius cloud platform.

While this all is great for Nebius, Nvidia’s goal here is not to make Nebius a monopoly. I don’t think it matters to Nvidia who this company is they are partnering with, and especially, the metrics that are important to us, as investors, are of no interest to Nvidia. Nvidia makes sure that as many "AI Factories" as possible are built using Nvidia technology, which is fair. They are essentially subsidizing their own future market, and I know they get criticized for that, but I don’t view this as a red flag, and you may read more about this in my previous article about NVDA. So, while this partnership gives Nebius important tools and a moat, it doesn't guarantee the execution, and this is not something exclusive. Nvidia will be partnering similarly with other companies, diversifying and creating a market for itself.

The Morgan Stanley And Earnings

At the Morgan Stanley conference, the CEO said that while they have massive contracts with companies like Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta (META), their goal is the rest of the market, meaning all of the startups and mid-sized enterprises they can get. Talking about this is very important for Nebius, as one of the largest current risks is the concentration. Relying on two customers is dangerous, and this is a strong bear case for this stock. I think the management recognizes it and tries to address those issues.

So yes, true. Everybody talks about our big contracts, about Microsoft contract, about Meta contract, billions of dollars. And those are great contracts, and those are great customers. But don't get distracted. Our main business model is not there. We are building a full-scale cloud for the rest of the AI builder market.

Besides, my main criticism of this company the last time was that the shadow of the company’s Russian origins will hang over it, potentially dragging the stock down. This risk is not widely discussed among analysts, and I did not see anyone else making this point.

At the same time, it looks like the company understands that this is a huge risk in itself, and to fight this, they are making "Westernization," which, I have to say, surprised me a lot. They hired Dan Lawrence (from AWS and Akamai) to run their US operations, and they got approval for a 1.2-gigawatt "AI factory" in Missouri. By hiring Western leadership and building physical infrastructure on U.S. soil, they are trying to prove to the U.S. government and Western corporations that their data is safe with them.

This is one of the main reasons I am moving to a Hold. They are doing the work to move past the geopolitical red flags I highlighted in December, and hopefully, the regulation will make this company really safe, hiding and protecting the data of its customers.

And today, we would build -- we mostly built ourselves. And this year, we are launching sites, which belong to us, which we built from scratch. And these are much bigger sites. This was the first example, Independence, Missouri. It's 800-megawatt, expandable to 1.2 gigawatt. Just got approval from the local communities. We have more sites coming like that. We build -- continue building in Europe. Majority of what we build now is in U.S., but we build not only in Europe and the U.S., we are now opening our offices in Middle East.

Another concerning part of Nebius is the capital expenditure (CapEx). Nebius plans to spend between $16 billion and $20 billion this year to build data centers and buy GPUs. To put that in perspective, the entire market value of the company is around $27 billion, and they only have about $3 billion in cash in the bank.

This is where the market should really be getting worried about CapEx. When people worry about Microsoft's huge CapEx (say, $150 billion this year), it’s worth remembering that Microsoft is a $3 trillion company (and has $90 billion in cash).

For Nebius to spend almost its market cap in one year is an incredibly aggressive, high-risk strategy, which should be taken into account before investing. They say 60% of this is covered by upfront payments from customers like Microsoft and Meta, but if something happens and they pull back, Nebius could be left with massive debt and a whole bunch of unfinished buildings. They are betting this whole company on the idea that AI demand will stay as strong, or will be even stronger, for at least the next 5 years. While I’m bullish on AI, and I believe the rally will continue for some more, I can’t say 5 years sounds right to me. I would give it another 2 years and then see where it goes, because while the market is strong right now, the capacity will sooner or later be overbuilt, and no one really knows when that time will come.

Valuation And Conclusion

On the recent earnings, 547% in revenue growth did not save the company from missing Q4 revenue estimates, and its net loss widened to $173 million. They are losing more money as they grow because the cost of building factories is very, very high. At the current valuation, the market has already priced in the good news, like the Nvidia deal, the Microsoft contract, and the U.S. expansion. This creates a very dangerous situation where the company needs everything to go perfectly just to keep the stock from going down.

Looking at the trailing EV to Sales ratio, we are seeing an insane 54x multiple, compared to the sector median of slightly over 3x. Even if we look at forward numbers, the company is trading at over 8x sales, which assumes that the massive $20 billion CapEx will translate into flawless revenue growth without any difficulties or problems, which is risky.

If there is even a delay in the Missouri plant or if Nvidia will be late with Rubin, the stock has a very long way to fall. A GAAP price-to-earnings ratio of over 942x, while not a very useful metric to look at for companies that are building out as fast as Nebius, still shows that investors are buying hopes of future earnings rather than the reality of today’s losses. Because the market has already priced in these catalysts, I don't see any significant upside left that hasn't been discussed. If the broader market begins to sell off companies that are overextended on AI spending, Nebius will likely be the most sold-off stock, and for a reason.

However, because they are taking the right steps with hiring Western leadership, I can no longer justify a Sell. I am upgrading Nebius to a Hold, but this is not a compelling Buy for me at this level, and the risks are still huge.

data.title: Heineken: Shares Still Cheap Amid Ongoing Demand Concerns
data.description:
Uncertainty being the theme of the day, I've been spending more time in the large-cap consumer staples department recently. Bargains aren't plentiful, but one name I continue to like is Dutch brewer Heineken N.V. (HEINY)(HINKF), whose shares still trade for a much lower price-to-earnings valuation than they did in the years preceding COVID.

Those of you that follow the company will rightly point out that this compression isn't entirely without merit. Weak demand is a real issue right now in the alcohol industry, and it remains to be seen how much is structural rather than cyclical.

Writing about Heineken last September, my view was that this was baked into its valuation. Back then, the stock was trading for 13.5x consensus 2025 EPS, whereas it typically averaged around 18-19x in the pre-COVID years when volume was growing at a decent clip.

Heineken's ADSs haven't moved much since then (they've returned around 3%), leaving its valuation basically unchanged. I continue to like the stock. Sure, there's some uncertainty here, but isn't that what typically creates fertile ground for potential investment? In a world in which the staples space trades for nearly 25x earnings, I think Heineken is offering patient investors a relatively good deal. For that reason, I'm sticking with my "Buy" rating.

Tepid Demand

Heineken reported 234.0 million hectoliters in beer volume last year. In absolute terms, this represented a decline of 2.8% compared to 2024 (it was a more modest 2.4% fall in organic terms). As you can see, volumes have been moribund for a while now.

One reason I have been sanguine about this trend is that the company's portfolio has been shifting more toward premium-priced brands. These include the Heineken namesake, and growth in this part of the business has been offsetting declines elsewhere. Last year was no different in this respect. While firm-wide beer volume fell 2.4% on an organic basis, premium volumes rose 2.7%. Branded Heineken grew by roughly the same amount.

Because these brews command a higher price per hectoliter, this mix shift is having a positive impact on Heineken's revenue, while the company has also been raising prices in the typical way. This combination led to €28.89 billion in underlying net sales last year. Although 3.7% lower compared to 2024, currency was a €1.47 billion headwind. Sales grew 1.6% on an organic basis.

Operating income is also rising, though again, this has been obscured by currency movements, which turned mid-single-digit organic growth into a reported decline of nearly 3% last year.

Heineken's margin was 15.2%, a 41 basis point improvement year-over-year. One thing I like about that performance is that it was not achieved by reducing marketing spending; the opposite, as the company bumped this line to the equivalent of 9.9% of revenue, a 10 basis point increase on 2024.

Let's turn back to volume for a moment. If you follow the alcohol space, you may have come across the narrative that demand has been impaired by several structural factors. These would include anti-obesity drugs, the rise of alternate product categories (the legalization of cannabis in certain developed markets, specifically), and changing attitudes/tastes among younger customers. Giving credence to this, in the USA, where you might reasonably expect this concoction to be most potent, industry-wide volumes fell mid-single-digits last year. Heineken's low-teens decline was even worse.

These may all be real headwinds. And yet, separating their impact from the more transient issues is where things get tricky. Inflation is the big one. In recent years, Heineken's pricing has outstripped the broader rise in CPI, inverting the pre-2020s relationship of below-inflation price hikes. The company also got embroiled in a pricing dispute last year with some retailers in Europe, which was a factor in its 3.4% volume decline there.

Reflecting this part-structural, part-temporary combination of headwinds, Heineken's management thinks that global beer volumes will grow 1% annualized through 2030. The company's operations skew to emerging markets with lower per capita consumption and more favorable socio-demographic trends, so its own prospects are arguably brighter than this. With contributions from pricing and mix, value growth should exceed volume growth.

That said, 2026 is not expected to be a rip-roaring year for the firm. Analysts only expect 1% organic volume growth, while Heineken's management is guiding for 2%-6% growth in operating profit. This was before the Iran War, the duration and impact of which remain to be seen. In other words, this is definitely a stock that could drift in the near term.

Discounted Valuation

Despite this, I come back to what I mentioned in the introduction about Heineken's valuation. At $40.94 as of this writing, the ADSs trade for just 13.65x the €5.24 per share analysts expect the firm to earn this year (that'd be around $3 per ADS at today's exchange rate). For reference, Heineken's year-end multiple averaged around 18.5x between 2010 and 2019 (because it spiked during COVID, it makes sense to exclude the 2020-2021 period from consideration).

This move is not masking a deterioration of the company's balance sheet. Heineken was sitting on €14.48 billion in net debt at the end of 2025, the equivalent of 2.2x adjusted EBITDA of €6.56 billion. As you can see, this is an entirely normal degree of leverage for the company (net debt has averaged around 2.5x EBITDA between 2010 and 2025). Cash generation also remains strong, with free operating cash flow of €2.60 billion easily covering the €1.05 billion cost of Heineken's dividend.

Based on last year's payout of €1.90 per share, the dividend yield is 2.7%. Here's how I look at things: to get to a reasonable 9% annualized return, Heineken needs to supplement its dividend with an extra 6.3% each year. Given a combination of volume growth, higher prices, premium-driven mix shift, margin expansion, and the impact of share buybacks, this does not seem aggressive. And with the price-to-earnings ratio being where it is, prospective buyers are well-placed to capture this business-level performance in terms of their own investment returns. Accordingly, I continue to rate shares "Buy".

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Deutsche Bank: Recent Pullback Provides A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

Deutsche Bank Aktiengesellschaft (DB) shares are off to a weak start in 2026, impacted by private credit exposures and the expected effects from the Iran war, which come against the backdrop of the ongoing conflict in Ukraine. While the bar for taking profits after DB's 2025 share price surge is not particularly high, the recent rout has restored DB's margin of safety, thus providing a buying opportunity for opportunistic investors.

I confirm my previous Buy rating on DB, with my bullish outlook underpinned by:

  • An undemanding 8.3x trailing P/E multiple, representing a circa 23% discount relative to the broader European banking sector.
  • Ambitious 2028 financial targets, potentially allowing for significant EPS growth if downside risks do not materialize.
  • A healthy 14.2% CET1 capital ratio and recently confirmed 2026 outlook, indicating little need to raise liquidity amid investor scrutiny of DB's private credit exposure.

Near-Term Headwinds

Most recently, DB came into news headlines regarding its €25.9 billion private credit exposure. In its 2025 annual report, DB elaborates:

Approximately 73% of this exposure is to multi-asset Lender facilities (ABS) collateralized by highly diversified mid-market corporate loans in the U.S. and the EU, across industry sectors, with conservative advance rates of ~65% and almost entirely investment grade rated. The remainder is diversified across single and multi asset lenders Net Asset Value (NAV) Financing, Single Asset Financing, non-bank CRE lending, business development companies (BDC) and subscription finance.

I should highlight that alongside its annual report publication, DB also confirmed its outlook for 2026, which most notably includes the assumption of a slight decline in the provisioning for credit losses. This is quite encouraging, as the statement comes after the outbreak of the war in Iran and increased market scrutiny on private credit markets.

In its annual reporting, DB does not explicitly break down its Middle East loan exposure. As such, assessing direct downside risks from the war in Iran is quite difficult. Looking at employee counts, however, Saudi Arabia stands at 52 and Qatar at only 4, which relative to the 95,980 headcount for DB as a whole is negligible.

To sum up, while better Middle East disclosure would be welcome in future reports, as things stand, DB's direct downside risks from the Iran war appear quite limited. As such, the bank's private credit exposure is a key near-term risk factor to watch. The good news is that with a CET1 ratio of 14.2% (above its 13.5-14.0% objective for 2028), DB is in no rush to raise liquidity. This is quite good, as current stress surrounding private credit appears to be focused more on the inability for investors to access their funds rather than underlying portfolio quality issues.

Medium-Term Opportunity

Aside from near-term risk factors highlighted above, DB appears confident in reaching a higher profitability in the next few years. In numbers, the bank expects to deliver a return on tangible equity (ROTE) of >13% by 2028, a significant improvement relative to the ROTE of 10.3% achieved in 2025. This is somewhat above the 12% ROTE I expected in my previous coverage of DB.

Where are the gains expected to come from? Firstly, as discussed in the presentation of CFO Raja Akram, DB aims to achieve better capital efficiency and a cost-income ratio of below 60% in 2028, allowing more of the anticipated 5% annual revenue growth to reach DB's bottom line.

If you look at European peers, a cost-income ratio of 60% is still quite high, indicating that even if DB manages to reach its targets, it would still rank as one of Europe's less efficient banks. For comparison, UniCredit (UNCFF) achieved a 38% cost/income ratio in 2025.

What are the key assumptions underpinning the 2028 profitability improvement? Well, DB has penciled in a 2.00% ECB deposit rate in 2026, gradually increasing to 2.5% in 2028. The bank also anticipated 1.1% GDP expansion in the Eurozone this year, jumping to 1.3% in 2028.

Against the backdrop of recent events, the assumptions regarding ECB policy appear reasonable (although I would note that if nothing changes, odds are for somewhat higher ECB rates in 2026 but lower in 2027-2028 as the effect of the energy price surge fades). European GDP growth, however, will likely be below DB's expectations, weighed down by high-energy costs and no end in sight for the war in Ukraine.

To sum up, on balance I see risks for DB's >13% ROTE target as tilted to the downside.

Valuation And Rating

While DB stock has naturally been quite volatile in recent weeks, as I write this, the shares trade at €25.61/share, representing an 8.3x trailing P/E multiple and a tangible book value discount of around 17%. This remains notably cheaper than direct European peers held in the iShares STOXX Europe 600 Banks UCITS ETF (DJXSF), which trade at roughly 10.83x their trailing earnings.

In light of DB's significant expected earnings growth and recently confirmed 2026 outlook, the circa 23% trailing P/E discount relative to peers appears excessive. The good news is that DB started its €1 billion share buyback on February 28, indicating the bank will be able to take advantage of the recent pullback in the share price. Investors can track progress and achieved share buyback prices on the DB website here. I estimate that the buyback will be enough to retire almost 2% of shares outstanding, providing a small boost to tangible book value thanks to the current discount.

Overall, I would say that a lot of gloom is currently reflected in DB shares. Hence, even if the bank ultimately underperforms its 2026 outlook and reports a higher cost of risk, the profitability hit is arguably priced in to an extent. As such, notwithstanding risks that I will discuss in the next section, I confirm my previous Buy rating on the shares.

Risks

Amid ongoing political tensions, weaker-than-expected GDP growth (associated with higher credit costs) is a key risk to consider. After several years of lackluster economic growth, the IMF had penciled in a 1.1% expansion in German GDP this year, accelerating to 1.5% in 2027. This will likely be revised down, potentially putting pressure on DB's provisioning.

While financial market volatility is arguably good for DB's trading business in fixed income and currencies, lower valuations will likely weigh on asset management fees collected by DB. Since DB targets a 2028 ROTE of >40% in asset management, persistently low stock and bond valuations (i.e., high fixed income yields) can also impact returns, thus undermining a key pillar of the bank's future profitability improvement.

Elevated uncertainty can also weigh on loan demand, leading DB to undershoot its 2% annual loan growth target in 2026-2028. This in turn could lead to the bank underperforming its net interest income growth expectations, resulting in a higher-than-expected cost/income ratio in 2028.

Conclusion

After a stellar 2025 for DB stock, 2026 has so far been characterized by profit taking, driven by DB's elevated private credit exposure and broader economic uncertainty.

Against this backdrop, it is encouraging to see DB confirming its 2026 targets, potentially indicating that the cost of risk should remain low. At the same time, ongoing share repurchases allow the bank to take advantage of share price weakness, potentially retiring almost 2% of shares outstanding.

While I see no DB-specific near-term catalysts for the share price, a resolution to the war in Iran and/or Ukraine could improve risk sentiment. This scenario is so far neglected by the market, allowing opportunistic investors to enter DB at a circa 23% discount to the broader European banking sector.

Key risks in the investment case include higher-than-expected credit loss provisioning, underperformance in the asset management segment, and lower-than-projected loan demand.

Thank you for reading.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Adobe: Record Quarter, But ARR Slowdown And CEO Transition Drag Stock Down
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Adobe Inc. (ADBE) just delivered a record quarter, beating revenue and earnings expectations while showing increased traction across its AI initiatives.

And yet the stock has sold off, down over 6% as I write this.

It seems like investors appear concerned about a slowdown in ARR growth and potentially the surprise announcement that CEO Shantanu Narayen will step down.

And yet, I see this as an opportunity for those willing to weather the storm.

AI is a threat to Adobe, but it is also an opportunity, and the company is making the right moves to become a long-term winner.

Interestingly, legendary investor Michael Burry apparently has initiated a position in Adobe. Burry likes to be a contrarian, and that’s certainly the case with Adobe right now.

Is Adobe the new PayPal (PYPL) or the next Alphabet (GOOGL)? This is what I asked in my last article, and the stock is down 27% since then.

But in the face of a record quarter and Michael Burry’s endorsement, I remain bullish.

A Strong Quarter That Somehow Disappointed

Adobe’s Q1 results were objectively solid.

The company achieved $6.4B in revenue in Q1, which represents a 12% YoY growth. On the earnings front, non-GAAP EPS came in at $6.06. This was actually a double beat by the company.

Profitability also remained very strong, with non-GAAP operating margins reaching 47.4%.

Yet the stock sold off after the earnings release.

The way most see it, this is likely due to two key reasons: the slowdown in ARR growth and the CEO's departure.

The knee-jerk reaction of the stock is often to trade off of the ARR figure, a metric that is rarely-if-ever disclosed by mega-cap software companies, and investors often struggle when they assess this metric,

Source: JPM Analyst, SA Press Release.

Now moving on to the CEO, leadership changes always introduce uncertainty, especially when the departing executive has overseen such a successful era.

During the call, Narayen acknowledged the transition while emphasizing the company’s continued focus on growth.

I will continue to lead Adobe as CEO and will stay on as Chair of the Board to support my successor.

Source: Earnings Call.

But as mentioned above, Narayen will remain chairman and assist with the transition, which reduces the risk of significant disruption.

The AI Narrative Is Being Misunderstood

The basic concern around Adobe is that generative AI could reduce the need for traditional creative software.

This is just one of many AI disruptions that the market is exaggerating, in my opinion.

Like many other SaaS companies, Adobe is not sitting on the sidelines watching this transformation happen.

Instead, the company is embedding AI directly into its products through its Firefly generative AI platform.

And message metrics suggest this strategy is working.

Generative credit consumption increased more than 45% quarter-over-quarter.

Source: Earnings Call.

Even more importantly, AI usage is moving beyond experimentation into real production workflows.

Video generative actions grew more than 8x year-over-year and audio generative actions doubled year-over-year.

Source: Earnings Call.

Clearly, AI is becoming integrated into the creative process, but it's not replacing it. If anything, this gives Adobe the chance to add more value to the final consumer.

The Freemium Strategy Is Masking Growth

What a lot of investors are also overlooking is Adobe’s expanding user base.

The company reported over 850 million monthly active users across its platforms, which represents roughly 17% YoY growth.

But as management explained, this surge in users is intentionally being driven through freemium distribution models.

These offerings have a near-term impact on ARR… but set the foundation for accelerated growth over time.

Source: Earnings Call.

This will be painful in the short term, but it’s the right approach to take in the face of AI disruption.

Valuation Is Compelling (Even for Burry)

And yet, the valuation just keeps sliding and looking increasingly attractive.

The company trades at roughly 11–12x forward earnings and a PEG below one.

This is unreasonable for a company still growing at double digits and with such strong margins.

Like with PayPal, it becomes a matter of sentiment right now, but eventually logic and pure cash flow should help turn Adobe’s price around.

Risks

But there are still plenty of potential risks with Adobe.

First and foremost, generative AI could still compress Adobe’s pricing power over time.

As AI tools become commoditized or competitors offer similar tools at lower cost, Adobe’s historically strong margins could face pressure.

Secondly, with the company currently transitioning toward a freemium user acquisition model, this delays monetization, and if Adobe struggles to convert its rapidly growing user base into paying subscribers, this could be a problem

Final Thoughts

The market hates Adobe right now, but it has found some love in Michael Burry. Is the legendary investor wrong, or just early again?

Personally, I think at this price, Adobe is a compelling Buy, even with the risks presented by AI.

data.title: Adobe: Fear Of A Value Trap
data.description:
Shares of Adobe (ADBE) have plunged to levels not seen since 2018, some eight years ago, as a testament to the degree of turmoil that AI has caused in the land of software. The news about long-time CEO Shantanu Narayen resigning and an unconvincing outlook, with no quick fixes to address the AI scare, triggered a new selling wave. Currently, shares are down about two-thirds from the peak in 2021.

The question is if this is really the transformative and bloodshed moment or if further dips will be seen. The pace of creative destruction is so rapid, yet it's hardly seen in the results. This creates compelling valuation multiples.

This raises the question about the degree and pace of disruption in relation to the current valuations, but fear about a rapid disruption raises the bar to get involved as well. I'm usually attracted to large pullbacks, some of which are covered in greater detail at Value In Corporate Events, but I'm holding back on this one.

The News

Other than highly anticipated first-quarter results, investors in Adobe were mostly digesting the news that Narayen, who served as Adobe's CEO for some 18 years, will resign after a successor is appointed. That news was quite unexpected, certainly given the challenges, or at least perceived challenges, faced by the business.

Looking at the results, the company reported a 12% increase in first-quarter revenues to $6.40 billion, 97% comprised of subscription services, complemented by tiny service and product revenue streams.

Subscription revenues rose by some 13%, as total revenues rose by 11% on a constant currency basis. About 70% of sales of the business are generated from creative and marketing professionals, growing 11% in constant currency terms, while the remaining business professional and consumer revenues were up as much as 15% on the same basis.

While the company reported an AI-first ARR that more than tripled over the year, no quantification of these numbers was announced. Adobe guided for modest sequential revenue growth, seeing second-quarter sales between $6.43 and $6.48 billion.

The company reported incredible profits with GAAP earnings of $1.9 billion, translating into GAAP earnings of $4.60 per share. While adjusted earnings are reported at $6.06 per share, that excludes a $1.25 per share stock-based compensation expense, which I do not like to adjust for. Somewhat concerning in the outlook is that despite sequential revenue growth, the same does not apply to earnings. GAAP earnings are seen between $4.35-$4.40 per share, with adjusted earnings seen between $5.80-$5.85 per share.

The company ended the quarter with $662 million in net cash despite $2.5 billion in buybacks over the quarter, used to retire 8.1 million shares at an average price of just over $300 per share. Likely a modest net debt load will be incurred going forward, given that Adobe has a pending deal to acquire Semrush, a $1.9 billion deal announced in November, as I expect a continuation of buybacks this quarter.

Valuations Implode

With 411 million shares now trading around the $250 mark, the market value of Adobe has effectively imploded to $100 billion, for a pretty similar enterprise valuation, as discussed above. This values the business at just 4 times annualized sales. GAAP earnings trend at $17-$18 per share, or could be seen a bit higher if we back out amortization charges, making the shares trade at 12 times GAAP earnings.

These are very cheap valuations, far cheaper than in the past, as Adobe, by my math, spent about $50 billion to acquire shares back over the past decade. This was enough to reduce the share count by nearly 100 million shares, on average having bought shares at much higher levels.

What Now?

The truth is that Adobe has been early in the buybacks, but fortunately, it managed balance sheet integrity on a net basis by being unleveraged. Currently, reported sales numbers look decent, yet investors are not just fearful about competition from cheaper and nimbler competitors like Figma and Canva; it really is the concerns around AI, as it appears that switching barriers are coming down as well.

While the company claims that new user numbers are gaining momentum, this is seen in growing freemium accounts. After all, a 50% increase in freemium MAU to 80 million looks impressive across various product suites. That suggests that ARPU is coming down, as upselling here will be very tough. The company furthermore reported an 850 million user base of its entire suite of products, which is up 17% on the year before.

This suggests that many users are using the products for free. In fact, if we apply total revenues to user numbers, this makes the ARPU just $7.50 per quarter, as a testament to the massive dispersion between total users and total paying user numbers.

The question is how quickly the disruption will happen, with second-quarter sales seen up 10%, although earnings will see some impact. The question of where this margin pressure comes from was not really discussed on the conference call. The market is clearly not convinced, and that creates a very interesting situation.

To be very bearish on the stock is very late in my view, down two-thirds from the highs, for a current industry leader operating with a strong balance sheet, still projecting growth, and trading at just about 12-13 times earnings. This looks too late to be bearish, as the real question is if now is the time to get involved on the long side.

While the numbers suggest a no-brainer, the market is clearly pricing in growth ending or reversing in the medium to long term. Other than the questions about disruption, there's an equally important question of the degree and pace at which this will happen. This is arguably key here, with the jury still out here.

Hence, I'm very interested given the valuation discussions, but I fear buying into the value trap, which raises the bar to get involved. I'm hoping to see another layer of peak pessimism to get somewhat of a better buffer for such outcomes.
